summary: Dan Luu analyzes leaked internal FTC memos from the 2011-2012 Google antitrust investigation, revealing how the Bureau of Economics (BE) made fundamental errors about the tech industry that contradicted the Bureau of Competition's (BC) case. BE staff argued mobile was unimportant, switching costs were trivial, and Google's anticompetitive practices had minimal impact—positions that were demonstrably wrong at the time and proven incorrect by subsequent events. The investigation was dropped despite BC making a defensible case, highlighting a systemic problem: tech policy decisions are made by people who lack basic understanding of the industry they're regulating.
key_points: The BE memo repeatedly made claims that were obviously false to anyone in tech, such as mobile being unimportant (8% of market) when it was already understood to be the future
BE staff used selective evidence and flawed models, like claiming defaults don't matter because switching takes 'a few taps,' while ignoring that Google paid billions for default status
The FTC gave equal weight to both memos despite BE's fundamental errors, suggesting tech expertise gaps at leadership levels
BE staff's predictions were systematically wrong—they predicted robust competition would continue, but Google's search share grew from ~67% to 86-90%
The lack of tech practitioners involved in crafting remedies and regulation allows companies to easily 'hack' around poorly-designed consent decrees
money_quotes: In order for the BE memo to seem credible, the reader must have large and significant gaps in their understanding of the tech industry.
When the authors of the BE memo make a prediction, they seem to have a facility for predicting the opposite of what will happen.
It's curious that someone could have access to all of these internal documents from these companies as well as interviews and then make the argument that mobile was, at the time, not very important.

We can see, from the history of antitrust in tech going back at least two decades, that courts, regulators, and legislators have not been prepared for the vigor, speed, and delight with which tech companies hack the system.

The BE memo argues that defaults are essentially worthless and have little to no impact, saying multiple times that users can switch with just 'a few taps'... Google paid Apple $26.3B to retain its default status in 2021.
key_insight: Effective tech regulation requires practitioners with hands-on industry expertise who understand how these systems actually work, not just economists and lawyers applying abstract models that contradict observable market behavior and incentives.
